summary:
The Development of Smallpox Vaccination (1796 AD) by Edward Jenner was one of the most important medical discoveries in history, the founding of immunology as a discipline, and the first step in the eventual global eradication of smallpox (achieved in 1980) — a disease that had killed an estimated 300–500 million people in the 20th century alone and had devastated populations worldwide. Jenner, a rural physician in Gloucestershire, England, noticed that milkmaids who had contracted the mild cowpox disease appeared to be immune to smallpox. On May 14, 1796, he inoculated the 8-year-old James Phipps with matter from cowpox pustules on the hands of the milkmaid Sarah Nelmes, then challenged him with variolous matter (smallpox inoculation) — and Phipps did not develop smallpox.

Jenner published his findings in 1798, introducing the term 'vaccine' (from vacca, the Latin for cow) to describe his procedure. The difference between vaccination and the earlier practice of variolation (inoculating people with actual smallpox material to induce mild immunity, practiced in the Ottoman Empire and introduced to Britain by Lady Mary Wortley Montagu in 1721) was crucial: vaccination used a related but harmless pathogen, making it far safer than variolation, which carried a real (if small) risk of causing fatal smallpox. Vaccination spread rapidly across Europe (Napoleon had his army vaccinated in 1805) and eventually worldwide. Though Jenner didn't understand the germ theory of disease (which awaited Pasteur and Koch in the 1860s–1880s), his empirical approach — observing a natural phenomenon, forming a hypothesis, testing it, and publishing with replicable methods — was a model of scientific practice. The WHO's global smallpox eradication campaign (1967–1980) built directly on Jenner's foundation.
